title: Epinephrine in Allergy: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Identify absolute indications for epinephrine in allergic reactions
Compare intramuscular vs intravenous epinephrine dosing
Address common barriers to epinephrine administration

sections.title: Mechanism of Action
sections.content:
**Epinephrine is the FIRST-LINE treatment for anaphylaxis**

• α1 agonism: Vasoconstriction → reverses hypotension and reduces angioedema
• β1 agonism: Increased heart rate and contractility
• β2 agonism: Bronchodilation → reverses bronchospasm
• Mast cell stabilization: Reduces further mediator release

**Onset:** 3-5 minutes IM | Immediate IV
**Duration:** 15-20 minutes (may need repeat dosing)

sections.title: Dosing & Routes
sections.content:
**Intramuscular (anterolateral thigh):**
• Adults: 0.3-0.5 mg of 1:1,000 (1 mg/mL)
• Pediatric: 0.01 mg/kg (max 0.3 mg)
• May repeat every 5-15 minutes

**Autoinjectors:**
• EpiPen: 0.3 mg (≥30 kg) or 0.15 mg (15-30 kg)
• May use through clothing

**Intravenous (refractory anaphylaxis):**
• 1:10,000 concentration (0.1 mg/mL)
• Adults: 0.1 mg slow push, may repeat
• NEVER give 1:1,000 concentration IV

sections.title: When to Give Epinephrine
sections.content:
**Absolute indications (give immediately):**
• Anaphylaxis (any severity)
• Suspected anaphylaxis with hypotension or respiratory compromise
• Known allergen exposure with any systemic symptoms

**Signs of anaphylaxis (2+ organ systems):**
• Skin: Urticaria, flushing, angioedema
• Respiratory: Wheezing, stridor, dyspnea
• Cardiovascular: Hypotension, tachycardia, syncope
• GI: Vomiting, cramping, diarrhea

**KEY: When in doubt, GIVE epinephrine. Delayed administration is the #1 cause of anaphylaxis deaths.**

sections.title: Biphasic Reactions
sections.content:
**5-20% of anaphylaxis episodes have a biphasic phase**

• Second wave 1-72 hours after initial reaction (most within 8 hrs)
• Risk factors: Severe initial reaction, delayed epinephrine, oral allergen
• Management: Observe 4-6 hours minimum after anaphylaxis
• Prescribe epinephrine for discharge
• Some guidelines recommend short course of oral corticosteroids

mcqs.stem: A patient undergoes skin prick testing to peanut. The wheal is 8mm with a negative control of 0mm and histamine control of 5mm. How should this result be interpreted?

options.text: Positive test — wheal ≥3mm greater than negative control indicates IgE sensitization
options.explanation: Per AAAAI/ACAAI practice parameters, a wheal ≥3mm greater than the negative control is considered positive. This indicates IgE sensitization but must be correlated with clinical history to diagnose true allergy.

options.text: Negative test — wheal must exceed histamine control to be positive
options.explanation: The positive threshold is compared to the NEGATIVE control (≥3mm difference), not the histamine control. The histamine control validates that the patient can mount a response.

options.text: Equivocal — requires serum specific IgE for confirmation
options.explanation: An 8mm wheal with 0mm negative control is clearly positive (8mm difference). Serum sIgE may complement but is not required for this interpretation.

options.text: Invalid test — the histamine control is too small
options.explanation: A histamine wheal of ≥3mm validates adequate skin reactivity. A 5mm histamine response is normal and validates the test.

references.title: Epinephrine in Anaphylaxis - WAO Position Paper
